title: Quiz: Density Matrices and Mixed States
description: Test your understanding of density matrices, purity, and entropy

questions.question: What is the purity Tr(ρ²) of a pure state?
questions.explanation: For a pure state ρ = |ψ⟩⟨ψ|, we have ρ² = ρ, so Tr(ρ²) = Tr(ρ) = 1.

questions.question: What is the von Neumann entropy of a pure state?
questions.explanation: Pure states have zero entropy. S = -Tr(ρ log ρ) = 0 because the only eigenvalue is 1, and 1·log(1) = 0.

questions.question: For a maximally mixed 2-qubit state ρ = I/4, what is the purity?
questions.explanation: Tr((I/4)²) = Tr(I/16) = 4/16 = 1/4. In general, maximally mixed state has purity 1/d.

questions.question: The density matrix ρ = 0.5|0⟩⟨0| + 0.5|1⟩⟨1| represents the same state as |+⟩ = (|0⟩+|1⟩)/√2.
questions.explanation: False! The first is a mixed state (classical mixture), while |+⟩ is a pure state (quantum superposition). They have different density matrices and different measurement statistics in the X basis.

questions.question: When you trace out one qubit of a Bell state |Φ+⟩ = (|00⟩+|11⟩)/√2, the reduced density matrix is:
questions.explanation: Tracing out one qubit of a maximally entangled state gives the maximally mixed state I/2. This is a signature of maximal entanglement.

questions.question: How do you calculate the expectation value ⟨A⟩ using a density matrix?
questions.explanation: ⟨A⟩ = Tr(ρA). This formula works for both pure and mixed states.

questions.question: A valid density matrix must satisfy: Hermitian, positive semidefinite, and Tr(ρ) = 1.
questions.explanation: True! These are the three requirements for a valid density matrix representing a physical quantum state.

questions.question: Which statements about the Bloch sphere representation are correct? (Select all)

options.text: Pure states lie on the surface (|r|=1)
Mixed states lie inside the sphere (|r|<1)
The maximally mixed state is at the center
All two-qubit states can be represented

questions.explanation: a, b, c are correct. The Bloch sphere only represents single-qubit states; two-qubit states require 15 real parameters and cannot be visualized on a simple sphere.
